one_line_takeaway: A rigorous 8-dimension benchmark (SecLLMHolmes, 228 scenarios, 8 models) shows state-of-the-art LLMs are not yet reliable security vulnerability detectors: high false positives, non-deterministic and unfaithful reasoning, and easily fooled by trivial code changes (26% wrong for PaLM2, 17% for GPT-4).
key_finding: No evaluated LLM is a reliable vulnerability detector. GPT-4 reaches at best ~89.5% accuracy (43/48) on hand-crafted scenarios, but all models show high false-positive rates (flagging patched code as vulnerable), non-deterministic outputs even at temperature 0.0, and frequently unfaithful reasoning (e.g. GPT-4: 14/810 correct-answer-with-wrong-reason). Robustness is the headline weakness: semantics-preserving code augmentations (renaming functions/variables, adding library functions) flip verdicts in 26% of cases for PaLM2 and 17% for GPT-4, and models are biased by security-suggestive identifiers and library-function names. All LLMs also fail on multiple 2023 real-world CVEs, where few-shot prompting does not help.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SecLLMHolmes, a fully automated, LLM-agnostic evaluation framework and benchmark suite for measuring whether chat-based LLMs can reliably identify and reason about security vulnerabilities in source code. The authors build a dataset of 228 code scenarios (48 hand-crafted vulnerable/patched pairs across 8 critical MITRE CWEs in C and Python, 30 real-world CVE scenarios from 15 2023 CVEs, and 150 code-augmentation scenarios) plus 17 prompt templates, and evaluate 8 LLMs across 8 investigative dimensions (deterministic response, parameter range, prompt diversity, faithful reasoning, vulnerability variety, code difficulty, robustness to augmentations, real-world use). The distinguishing claim vs. prior work (their Table 2) is that theirs is the first framework to go beyond binary vulnerable/not-vulnerable labels and also automatically evaluate the LLM's reasoning/root-cause quality and code-level robustness. Note: supervised-ML detectors (VulDeePecker, VulChecker, VulBERTa, UniXCoder, Joern, etc.) and the Risse et al. robustness finding are cited Related Work, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: This is an evaluation/benchmark paper rather than an attack paper, but it squarely concerns ML system safety/robustness: it studies whether LLMs deployed as security assistants for vulnerability detection are trustworthy, and demonstrates a robustness weakness in which semantics-preserving code augmentations (renaming functions/variables, adding red-herring library functions, whitespace/newline insertion, misleading sanitizer names) act as an adversarial perturbation that flips the model's verdict. The implied risk is that using such LLMs in production would produce unreliable detections and a flood of false positives, and that adversarial or incidental code patterns could mask real vulnerabilities.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: the Evaluator relies on GPT-4 to extract answers/reasoning (2/100 mis-formatted in manual audit) and on a 3-metric majority vote for reasoning that can still agree on a wrong output (7/100 false positives observed). Evaluating newer models requires curating post-cutoff CVEs to avoid contamination. Code-scenario coverage is limited in difficulty levels, augmentation types, languages, and vulnerability classes. Additional reviewer-noted limits: only 8 CWEs and 2 languages (C/Python); only 5 of 8 LLMs are detailed in the main body; real-world code was truncated to fit token limits, which may remove context; models are the late-2023 generation (GPT-4, GPT-3.5, PaLM2, CodeLlama, StarChat), so findings are a snapshot ('yet?' in the title).

datasets_used: 228 total code scenarios built by the authors

48 hand-crafted vulnerable/patched scenarios across 8 MITRE Top-25 (2023) CWEs: CWE-787, CWE-79, CWE-89, CWE-416, CWE-22, CWE-476, CWE-190, CWE-77 (C and Python), 3 difficulty levels (easy/medium/hard)
30 real-world CVE scenarios (15 CVEs, vulnerable+patched) from gpac, libtiff, linux, pjsip, all published and fixed in 2023 (post model cutoff), truncated to <=6144 tokens
150 code-augmentation scenarios (84 trivial via 7 augmentations on CWE-787/CWE-89; 66 non-trivial via 6 augmentations)
48 author-written ground-truth reasoning explanations (Fleiss kappa 0.93) + 180 by first author
quantitative_results: GPT-4 best/max accuracy ~89.5% on 48 hand-crafted scenarios (43/48 on prompts D4, R5, D5)
Code-augmentation non-robustness: PaLM2 gives incorrect answers in 26% of cases and GPT-4 in 17% when function/variable names change or library functions are added
All models non-deterministic at recommended temperature 0.2; at temperature 0.0 codechat-bison, codellama34b and gpt-3.5 became consistent but chat-bison and gpt-4 remained inconsistent over 10 runs

Faithfulness (Table 12, out of scenarios answered): gpt-4 612 correct-answer+correct-reason, 14 correct-answer+wrong-reason, 168 wrong+wrong, 16 wrong-answer+correct-reason; gpt-4 and codellama34b give a reason for every answer (810/810 and 814/814); PaLM2 models have lower reason rates (chat-bison 724/782, codechat-bison 680/719)
Evaluator validation on 50 pairs: ROUGE threshold 0.34 (43/50 consistent), cosine-sim threshold 0.84, GPT-4 judge correct 48/50
Few-shot prompting significantly beats zero-shot (p=0.003); role-oriented slightly beats task-oriented (p=0.1)
All LLMs produce incorrect answers on several real-world CVEs and frequently flag patched code as vulnerable (high false-positive rate); few-shot prompting does not help on real-world scenarios
baselines_compared: Eight LLMs evaluated and compared against each other: gpt-4, gpt-3.5-turbo-16k, PaLM2 codechat-bison@001, PaLM2 chat-bison@001, CodeLlama-7B-instruct, CodeLlama-13B-instruct, CodeLlama-34B-instruct, StarChat-beta
Prior evaluation frameworks compared qualitatively in Table 2: Vulnerable Code Generation [8], Vulnerable Code Repair [7], Limits of ML [30] (Risse et al.), Transferable Vulnerability Detection [34]
Referenced-but-not-run prior detectors (Related Work only): Joern, VulChecker, VulDeePecker, SySeVR, VulBERTa, UniXCoder, CoText, Meta Pysa
